The resolution string `res` is added unconditionally, but when resolution is null, `res` will be empty, causing unwanted underscores or empty segments (e.g., "TEST(SPY_)" instead of "TEST(SPY)").

The new logic always adds `res` to the parts list even when it's an empty string (which happens when resolution is null at line 1809). The old code used `.Where(p => !string.IsNullOrEmpty(p))` to filter out empty strings. This change breaks the behavior for cases like VWAP which calls `CreateIndicatorName(symbol, "VWAP", null)` at line 1642.

Add test cases with null resolution to verify the behavior when resolution is not provided, as the VWAP indicator uses this pattern.

The test suite doesn't cover the case where resolution is null, which is used in the codebase (e.g., VWAP at line 1642 in QCAlgorithm.Indicators.cs). This would help catch the bug where empty resolution strings are being added to the parts list.

Missing closing quote in href attribute - should be `#use_extension\">` instead of `#use_extension>`.

This is a malformed HTML link that will not render correctly in the generated documentation. The href attribute is missing its closing quote.

Missing space at end of line - should be `"all "` or next line should start with `" repositories"` to avoid "allrepositories".

When these strings are concatenated, "all" directly joins with "repositories" creating "allrepositories" instead of "all repositories".

Missing space at end of line - should be `"all "` or next line should start with `" repositories"` to avoid "allrepositories".

When these strings are concatenated, "all" directly joins with "repositories" creating "allrepositories" instead of "all repositories".

This should be `(*streams_encoded)++` to increment the value pointed to by the pointer, not the pointer itself.

The parameter `streams_encoded` is declared as `uint8_t *streams_encoded` (a pointer). The current code `streams_encoded++` increments the pointer address, not the value it points to. This means the caller's variable won't be updated correctly, leading to incorrect indexing into `stream_data` array in the `encode_base` function.

Add a check for `stream_param->data != NULL` before calling memcpy when `data_count > 0`, or skip the memcpy when `data_count` is 0.

If `stream_param->data` is NULL and `stream_param->data_count` is non-zero, this memcpy will cause undefined behavior. The validation function `valid_create_param` doesn't validate the consistency of the `data` and `data_count` fields in stream parameters.

Add a check to ensure `subgroup->streams` is not empty before calling `sys_slist_peek_head`, otherwise `head_node` will be NULL and `CONTAINER_OF` will produce an invalid pointer.

If a subgroup somehow has no streams (although this shouldn't happen based on the creation logic), this code will crash. Adding a defensive check would prevent potential NULL pointer dereference.

The error message should include the total stream count that would exceed the limit: `subgroup_param->params_count + stream_count` instead of just `stream_count`.

The current message only shows `stream_count` which is the number of streams added so far, not the total that would be created. This makes debugging harder as the error message doesn't clearly indicate what the actual problematic total would be.

The `stream_count` field is no longer being updated or used after the refactoring, but it remains in the struct. Either remove this field or ensure it's properly maintained.

The old code populated `source->stream_count` during creation, but the new code using subgroups doesn't set this field. Since the stream count can now be derived by iterating through subgroups and their streams, this field appears to be dead code.

`broadcast_source_get_state(source)` is called twice - use the value already stored in `broadcast_state`.

The function is called once and stored in broadcast_state on line 757, but then called again in the if condition on line 758. This is inefficient and should use the stored value instead.

`broadcast_source_get_state(source)` is called twice - use the value already stored in `broadcast_state`.

The function is called once and stored in broadcast_state on line 791, but then called again in the if condition on line 792. This is inefficient and should use the stored value instead.

The range minimum was changed from 0 to 1, but this could be a breaking change if any configurations set this to 0 to disable broadcast source support.

Changing the range minimum from 0 to 1 means configurations that previously disabled this feature by setting it to 0 will now fail. Consider whether this is intentional or if backward compatibility should be maintained.

Integer division could result in uneven distribution of streams across subgroups if the stream count is not evenly divisible by the subgroup count. Consider handling the remainder or documenting this assumption.

For example, if CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_STREAM_COUNT=2 and CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT=2, this works fine with 1 stream per subgroup. However, if someone configures 3 streams and 2 subgroups, `streams_per_subgroup` would be 1, leaving one stream unused. The sample should either handle this or document the constraint.

The entire BIS codec data loop is wrapped in `#if defined(CONFIG_BT_CODEC_MAX_DATA_COUNT)`, which means when this config is not defined, the length field will always be 0 even if `stream_data` has codec data. Consider whether this is the intended behavior or if the config should guard the `struct bt_audio_broadcast_stream_data` definition instead.

This conditional compilation could lead to inconsistency where BIS-specific codec data is stored in `stream_data` during creation but never encoded in the BASE if CONFIG_BT_CODEC_MAX_DATA_COUNT is not defined. The struct definition in endpoint.h already conditionally compiles the data fields, so this additional guard may be redundant or the logic should be reconsidered.

Each subgroup is pointing to a single stream_params element at index `i`, but with multiple subgroups this creates overlapping/incorrect stream assignments. Subgroup 0 gets stream_params[0], subgroup 1 gets stream_params[1], etc., which may not be the intended distribution.

The test creates CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT subgroups (configured as 1 in prj.conf) but the logic assumes each subgroup gets one stream starting from stream_params[i]. If SUBGROUP_COUNT were increased, this would lead to each subgroup having only 1 stream instead of distributing all streams across subgroups properly. This seems inconsistent with the sample code which divides streams evenly.

Using codec from `preset_16_2_1` but QoS from `preset_16_2_2` appears inconsistent. Verify this is intentional for testing purposes or if both should use the same preset.

Mixing codecs and QoS from different presets could lead to configuration mismatches. If this is for testing compatibility between different presets, it should be documented; otherwise, it's likely an error and should use the same preset for both.

The subgroup is not initialized before being returned, which means `subgroup->codec` and `subgroup->_node` may contain garbage values. Add initialization similar to `broadcast_source_ep_init`.

When a subgroup is reused after being freed, it may contain stale data. The `sys_slist_is_empty` check only verifies that the streams list is empty, but doesn't guarantee the subgroup structure is in a clean state. This could lead to issues if the subgroup was previously used and not properly cleaned up.

After removing streams and subgroups from their lists, the subgroup structures should be memset to 0 to ensure clean reuse, similar to how the source is cleared.

The cleanup only clears the source structure with memset, but the individual subgroup structures that are part of the `broadcast_source_subgroups` static array are not cleared. This leaves the subgroup->codec pointer and other fields in an undefined state, which ties into the earlier comment about initialization in `broadcast_source_new_subgroup`.

The `data` and `data_count` fields of `stream_params` are not initialized, leaving them as NULL/0. Consider explicitly setting these fields or documenting that BIS-specific codec data is not used in the shell.

After the memset, the stream_params are initialized with stream pointers but the `data` and `data_count` fields remain 0/NULL. While this is valid (BIS-specific codec data is optional), it would be clearer to explicitly show this intent, especially since the sample and test code do set these fields.

Add validation for `stream_param->data` and `stream_param->data_count` consistency to prevent NULL pointer dereference in memcpy during creation.

The validation should check that if `data_count > 0`, then `data` must not be NULL, and vice versa. This would catch configuration errors early rather than crashing during the memcpy operation at line 710.

Fix typo: "neccesarry" should be "necessary".

There's a spelling error in the documentation comment. While this typo existed in the original code, this PR is updating documentation, making it an ideal time to fix it.

Use proper capitalization: "oauth" should be "OAuth".

OAuth is a proper noun/acronym and should be capitalized consistently throughout the documentation.

The function name 'asFixer' is unclear and doesn't indicate that it returns -1 as a sentinel value to skip the fixed/count display.

The function name could be more descriptive, such as `wrapFixerWithoutCount` or similar, to indicate its purpose more clearly.

Using -1 as a magic sentinel value is fragile; consider using a boolean flag in the struct to indicate whether the fixer returns a count.

Magic numbers like -1 for sentinel values make the code harder to understand and maintain. A more explicit approach would be to add a field like `ReturnsCount bool` to the consistencyCheck struct.

The Fixer returns -1 but DeleteOrphanedObjects doesn't return the number of rows affected, which could be useful for logging.

Consider modifying DeleteOrphanedObjects to return the number of rows affected instead of just error, which would provide better feedback and eliminate the need for the -1 sentinel value.

Add parentheses around the date comparison logic for clarity: `or (task.start_date <= self.execution_date and ...)`.

While the operator precedence is correct (and binds tighter than or), adding explicit parentheses would make the intended logic clearer to readers and prevent potential misunderstandings about the conditions being evaluated together.

The comment should be clarified to "all tasks have end_date" for better readability.

The comment "not exists null end_date" is grammatically unclear and could be confusing to readers. A clearer comment would be "all tasks have end_date" or "no task has null end_date".

The test should also verify the explicit schema case from the original issue: `pl.from_records([{"a": 9908227375760408577}], schema={"a": pl.UInt64})`.

The original issue reported in the custom instructions shows that the problem occurs both with and without an explicit schema. The test only covers automatic type inference but should also verify that providing an explicit UInt64 schema preserves the value correctly, as mentioned in the issue description.

Use UINT64_MAX instead of INT32_MAX for initializing best_diff_nsec to ensure all valid differences will be smaller.

The variable best_diff_nsec is uint64_t, so it should be initialized to UINT64_MAX, not INT32_MAX. Using INT32_MAX could cause issues if the actual difference exceeds this value, preventing the algorithm from finding a match.

The brief description and parameter documentation are inconsistent; this function converts a single frame interval to nanoseconds, not microseconds, and doesn't compute a difference.

The documentation says "Compute the difference between two frame intervals" and mentions "microseconds" in the param/return docs, but the function actually converts one frame interval to nanoseconds. The comment should be corrected to match the actual behavior.

Remove the duplicate assertion on line 73; line 74 already checks the same condition with a message.

There are two consecutive zassert_not_ok calls testing the same value. The first one on line 73 is redundant and should be removed.

Clarify the assertion message to better explain that this function finds a discrete value, not a stepwise range.

The current message "cannot find range matching the range, only a value matching the range" is confusing. A clearer message would be something like "match->type must be VIDEO_FRMIVAL_TYPE_DISCRETE, not STEPWISE".

Set match->type to VIDEO_FRMIVAL_TYPE_DISCRETE when a match is found, as callers expect this field to be set in the result.

The function modifies match->discrete and match->index but never sets match->type. Based on the documentation and the assertion at line 134-135 that requires input type to be DISCRETE, the output type should also be set to VIDEO_FRMIVAL_TYPE_DISCRETE when updating the match.

Add overflow checks or document the assumption that denominator values are small enough to prevent uint64_t overflow in these multiplications.

The multiplications of three or four uint32_t values could theoretically overflow a uint64_t if the denominators are large. Either add overflow protection or document the expected range of input values to ensure this is safe.

Initialize fie.index to 0 explicitly for clarity, as video_enum_frmival expects index-based enumeration starting at 0.

While the struct initialization should zero-initialize all unspecified fields, explicitly setting fie.index = 0 makes the enumeration pattern clearer and more maintainable.

Handle the case where no frame intervals are enumerated; currently match->index would be uninitialized if the loop never executes.

If video_enum_frmival never returns 0 (no frame intervals available), the function exits without setting match->index or match->type, leaving them in an undefined state. Consider initializing match->index to 0 before the loop or documenting this edge case behavior.

Add validation that match->format is not NULL before using it.

The function dereferences match->format when assigning to fie.format, but doesn't validate it's non-NULL. According to the documentation, match->format must be set by the caller, so either add an assertion or a runtime check.

Add a test case for video_closest_frmival() to ensure complete API coverage.

The test suite covers video_format_caps_index, video_frmival_nsec, and video_closest_frmival_stepwise, but there's no test for the main video_closest_frmival function. Since this is a public API function, it should have test coverage to verify its behavior with discrete and stepwise frame intervals.

Use direct struct assignment instead of memcpy for better readability: match->discrete = tmp;

Since both are struct video_frmival types, direct assignment is clearer and more idiomatic than memcpy. The compiler will handle the copy efficiently.

The parameter name `rowIdx` is misleading because it receives different value types depending on the caller: for LOAD DATA it's already 1-based (from `e.rowCount`), but the name suggests a 0-based index. Consider renaming it to `rowNum` to clarify it expects a 1-based row number.

This inconsistency between parameter naming and actual usage can lead to confusion and bugs. The parameter is called `rowIdx` suggesting an index (0-based), but it actually expects a row number (1-based) when called from LOAD DATA context.

`completeLoadErr` only handles `ErrDataTooLong` but ignores other error types like `ErrOverflow`, `ErrTruncatedWrongVal`, etc. that `completeInsertErr` handles. This could lead to inconsistent error messages for LOAD DATA when these other errors occur.

The function should handle all the same error types that completeInsertErr handles to ensure consistent error reporting for LOAD DATA statements. Missing error type handling could result in less informative error messages.

Pass `on_behalf_of: current_account` to ensure proper authorization checks in ResolveURLService.

The ResolveURLService accepts an `on_behalf_of` parameter which is used for authorization checks (see line 28 in resolve_url_service.rb: `authorize_with @on_behalf_of, status, :show?`). Without passing the current account, authorization checks will be skipped, potentially allowing access to resources the user shouldn't see.

Add `response.headers['Vary'] = 'Cookie'` to prevent cache poisoning since the response differs based on authentication status.

The redirect behavior is different for authenticated vs unauthenticated users (authenticated users get URL resolution, unauthenticated don't). Without a Vary header, proxies and CDNs might cache an authenticated response and serve it to unauthenticated users or vice versa, leading to incorrect redirects or privacy leaks.

Validate the URL format to prevent redirecting to invalid or potentially dangerous URLs.

The URL parameter comes directly from user input and is used in a redirect without validation. Adding a check like `URI.parse(@url)` with error handling would prevent malformed URLs from causing errors or unexpected behavior. This is particularly important for the security-sensitive open redirect functionality.

Store the original href before modification to prevent encoding the already-modified URL if the link element is reused.

While the 'status-link' class check prevents reprocessing in most cases, if the DOM is manipulated externally or the element is reused, `link.href` could already be the `/redirect?url=...` format, leading to double-encoding. Storing the original href in a data attribute first would make this more robust.

Store the original href before modification to prevent encoding the already-modified URL if the link element is reused.

Similar to header.jsx, if this code runs multiple times on the same link element (e.g., on component updates), `link.href` could already be the `/redirect?url=...` format, leading to double-encoding. Consider storing the original href in a data attribute or checking if it's already been modified.

Add a note explaining why this open redirect is intentional and what mitigations are in place.

The empty note field should document that this open redirect is intentional for the feature to work (redirecting users to resolved posts/profiles or external URLs), and mention any mitigations like URL validation or rate limiting that protect against abuse.

Missing `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;` - The type `IFormFile` used in `ResumeUpload` record requires this namespace import.

The diff shows the removal of `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;` but `IFormFile` is still used on line 16 in the `ResumeUpload` record definition, which will cause a compilation error.

The condition `jsonTypeInfo.Type is Type type` is redundant since `jsonTypeInfo.Type` is already of type `Type`.

The `JsonTypeInfo.Type` property is already of type `Type`, so the pattern matching is unnecessary and adds no value. The check can be removed entirely.

If `TypeInfoResolver` is null, this will set the property to null which could cause runtime errors when creating schemas.

The null-conditional operator on line 38 means if the original TypeInfoResolver is null, the new JsonSerializerOptions will have a null TypeInfoResolver. This could cause issues in JsonSchemaMapper when trying to generate schemas. Consider using a null-coalescing operator to provide a default resolver.

The non-null assertion operator is unsafe here; if `find()` returns undefined, this will throw at runtime.

The `find()` method can return undefined if no element matches the predicate. Using the non-null assertion operator (!) without checking could cause a runtime error when accessing `.value`. Additionally, invalid IANA timezone names passed to `Intl.DateTimeFormat` will throw a RangeError that is not caught, causing the entire formatting to fail. Consider wrapping this logic in a try-catch block and handling errors gracefully by falling back to the original behavior.

Typo: should be "forward slash" not "forward-dash" since the character being checked is '/'.

The comment mentions "forward-dash" but the code checks for '/' which is a forward slash. A dash would be '-'. This should be corrected for accuracy.

`replace()` only replaces the first occurrence; use a regex with the global flag if multiple minus symbols need replacement.

The `replace()` method without a regex only replaces the first occurrence of the matched string. While timezone offsets typically have only one minus symbol, using `.replace(/\u2212/g, '-')` would be more robust and handle edge cases where multiple Unicode minus symbols might appear.

The check `timezone.includes('/')` may incorrectly identify invalid strings like 'abc/def' as IANA timezones, leading to errors.

Using `includes('/')` to detect IANA timezone names is a weak heuristic. Any string containing a forward slash will enter this code path, which could cause `Intl.DateTimeFormat` to throw a RangeError for invalid timezone names. Consider wrapping the Intl API call in a try-catch block to handle invalid timezones gracefully and fall back to the existing offset parsing logic.

Remove the unnecessary `.clone()` call since `options_preview` is not used after this point.

The `options_preview` variable is not used after being passed to `ensure_stability_when_formatting_twice`, so cloning it is wasteful. Since the function takes `PyFormatOptions` by value, we can pass `options_preview` directly instead of `options_preview.clone()`.

The pattern 'idle' will match any string containing "idle" (e.g., "idler", "not-idle"). Use '^idle$' to match only the exact string "idle".

The regex pattern 'idle' without anchors will match substrings, allowing invalid values to pass validation. For example, "idler" or "not-idle" would incorrectly pass validation. The pattern should be '^idle$' to ensure exact matching.

The condition should check `forceCloseConnections === true` for consistency, since line 197 uses strict equality. Using truthy check here accepts 'idle' which would be incorrect.

At line 403-404, the condition `serverHasCloseAllConnections && forceCloseConnections` uses a truthy check. While this works because 'idle' is handled earlier, it's clearer and safer to use `forceCloseConnections === true` to match the strict equality check on line 197 and make the intent explicit that only boolean true should trigger this branch.

Missing cleanup of syscall_log hashmap. Add: c->syscall_log = hashmap_free(c->syscall_log);

The header value should also strip trailing whitespace for consistency with HTTP standards.

HTTP header values typically have both leading and trailing whitespace trimmed according to RFC 7230. Currently, only leading whitespace is removed from the value using skip_leading_chars(), while the name gets full trimming via strstrip(). This inconsistency could lead to unexpected behavior with header values containing trailing spaces.

Calling strlen() twice on the same string is inefficient; store the result in a variable.

The strlen() function is called twice in this condition check. This is a small performance inefficiency that can be easily avoided by storing the length in a local variable.

Variable declarations should be at the beginning of the block for C89/C90 compatibility with systemd conventions.

In the systemd codebase, variable declarations are typically placed at the beginning of a code block rather than in the middle. This follows more traditional C conventions and ensures better compatibility.

Header value validation is missing space and tab characters, but whitespace is stripped before validation, creating inconsistency.

The VALID_HEADER_VALUE_CHARS constant includes ":" but not space or tab characters. However, in config_parse_header(), only leading whitespace is removed using skip_leading_chars(). This means: 1) Values with trailing spaces will fail validation, 2) Values with spaces in the middle cannot be used at all. HTTP header values commonly contain spaces (e.g., "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux)"), so this validation is too restrictive.

This test doesn't verify that custom headers are actually sent, only that the service works with the header configuration.

The integration test configures a custom header but doesn't validate that the header is actually included in HTTP requests. It only verifies that journal data is transferred successfully, which would work even without the custom header feature. A proper test would need to inspect the HTTP requests or use a mock server that validates received headers.

Missing test coverage for header names exceeding VALID_HEADER_NAME_LENGTH (40 characters) and values with invalid characters.

The unit test doesn't verify that header names longer than 40 characters are rejected, nor does it test header values with invalid characters. These are important edge cases that should be covered to ensure the validation logic works correctly.

According to HTTP standards, header names with underscores may be rejected by some servers; consider allowing only hyphens.

While RFC 7230 technically allows underscores in header field names, many HTTP servers (including nginx by default) and proxies reject or ignore headers containing underscores due to security and compatibility concerns. For better interoperability, consider restricting header names to alphanumeric characters and hyphens only, which is the common practice.

Replace hardcoded `0.1` with `exposure` to match directional light behavior (line 294).

Point and spot lights use a hardcoded multiplier of 0.1 instead of the exposure variable that directional lights use. This creates inconsistent brightness behavior between light types.

Calculate and apply phase using `henyey_greenstein()` like directional lights do (line 228-229), removing unused variables V and L.

Point and spot lights don't calculate the Henyey-Greenstein phase function that directional lights use, which is important for physically-based volumetric scattering. Variables V and L are defined but never used.

Change to `light.volumetric && light.shadows_enabled` to match the flag-setting condition at line 737-738.

The filter uses OR but the VOLUMETRIC flag is only set when both conditions are true (line 737-738). This mismatch causes incorrect counting and index range checking for volumetric point lights.

Add density texture sampling here like directional lights do (lines 245-257) to support density textures.

Point and spot lights don't sample the density texture when DENSITY_TEXTURE is defined, unlike directional lights which properly handle it. This causes inconsistent volumetric fog density.

Replace `point_light_volumetric_enabled_count` with `point_light_count` to correctly offset spot light indices.

Spot lights need their index offset by the total point light count, not the volumetric point light count. This matches the shadow map logic at line 728 and ensures correct index-based filtering for spot lights.

RecordObject should record `result` (the Stack), not `array`, to properly handle circular references.

The placeholderReferenceId was created for the Stack being deserialized, but the code incorrectly records the intermediate array instead of the final Stack result. This breaks reference tracking - if the serialized data contains circular references to the stack, deserialization will fail. Following the pattern in QueueCodec.cs (lines 80, 99) and ListCodec.cs (lines 88, 107), the actual collection result should be recorded, not intermediate data structures.

Add bounds checking: verify `i >= 0` before decrementing to prevent array index out of bounds exceptions.

If malformed data contains more elements than the declared length, the code will attempt to access negative array indices, causing an IndexOutOfRangeException. Adding a check like `if (i < 0) ThrowIndexOutOfRangeException(array.Length);` before line 98 would prevent this issue.

The generic type parameter should be `ModelInputForCPUWithSamplingMetadata` to match `_model_input_cls` and align with XPUModelRunner's pattern.

Returning a raw pointer to a WinRT object is unsafe and can lead to lifetime issues; consider returning a reference or using a smart pointer type.

The GetTabRow() method returns a raw pointer to a WinRT object (_tabRow), which is risky because the caller has no ownership guarantees. If the TerminalPage is destroyed while the caller still holds this pointer, it becomes a dangling pointer. While this is acknowledged as a workaround, consider using winrt::com_ptr or returning by reference to make the lifetime semantics clearer and safer.

The umount logic may fail if the VFS object passed is not in the global mount table or if readblocks[1] doesn't uniquely identify the filesystem.

The IV buffers must be zeroed before copying when ivlen < ctx->ivlen to prevent uninitialized data in the padding bytes.

When a shorter IV is provided (ivlen < ctx->ivlen), only the first ivlen bytes of ctx->iv and ctx->oiv are copied, leaving the remaining bytes potentially containing stale data from previous operations if the context is reused. This violates the LSB zero-padding requirement. The buffers should be zeroed with memset before memcpy, or use a pattern like: memset(ctx->iv, 0, ctx->ivlen); memcpy(ctx->iv, iv, ivlen); (same for ctx->oiv).

Add a null check for `zfp->mp->fs` before dereferencing it to prevent potential null pointer exceptions if unmount occurs concurrently.

While the PR removes the line that sets `mp->fs = NULL` in fs_unmount(), the custom instructions explicitly state that "the fs pointers must be NULL checked before dereference to protect in this case. This should be done in all functions that invoke fs specific calls." The CHECKIF macro dereferences `zfp->mp->fs` to check if `->close` is NULL, which could still crash if `zfp->mp->fs` itself is NULL. Adding a check like `if (zfp->mp->fs == NULL) return -ENODEV;` before the CHECKIF would provide defense-in-depth protection.

Add a null check for `zfp->mp->fs` before dereferencing it to prevent potential null pointer exceptions if unmount occurs concurrently.

Per the custom instructions, all functions that invoke fs specific calls should check fs pointers before dereference. This function dereferences `zfp->mp->fs` in the CHECKIF macro without first verifying it's not NULL, which could cause a crash in race conditions.

Add a null check for `zfp->mp->fs` before dereferencing it to prevent potential null pointer exceptions if unmount occurs concurrently.

Per the custom instructions, all functions that invoke fs specific calls should check fs pointers before dereference. This applies to fs_read, fs_seek, fs_tell, fs_truncate, fs_sync, and other file operations that dereference `zfp->mp->fs` without first checking if it's NULL.

The mutex protection only covers the mount list operations, not ongoing file I/O operations that may be accessing mp->fs concurrently.

The custom instructions state "the fs API needs to be fully mutex protected". While this fix prevents the NULL pointer issue by not clearing mp->fs, there's still a race condition where file I/O operations (fs_read, fs_write, fs_close, etc.) can access mp->fs without holding the mutex while fs_unmount is executing. This could lead to undefined behavior depending on the underlying filesystem driver implementation.

Commenting out this test removes coverage for recursive update behavior. Instead, either assert the expected exception or document this as a known limitation with a test annotation like @Ignore with an explanation.

The method no longer returns the created dag_runs list, which could break any code expecting a return value.

This list comprehension with [0] indexing will raise an IndexError if no matching task instance is found, which could happen if describe_tasks returns stale tasks not in the input tis list.

The code assumes that every task returned by describe_tasks has a corresponding task instance in the input tis list. However, describe_tasks could potentially return tasks that were launched by a previous scheduler instance but are not in the current adoption list. This would cause an IndexError. The code should use a safer approach, such as using next() with a default value or filtering to only process tasks that have a matching TI.

The test mocks are incomplete - they're missing required attributes (key, queue, command_as_list, executor_config) that are accessed in try_adopt_task_instances, which will cause the test to fail.

The try_adopt_task_instances method calls ti.key, ti.queue, ti.command_as_list(), ti.executor_config, and ti.prev_attempted_tries. The test only sets external_executor_id and prev_attempted_tries, leaving the other attributes unset. When using mock.Mock(spec=TaskInstance), accessing these attributes may work but will return new Mock objects with unpredictable behavior. The test should explicitly set or mock these attributes to ensure deterministic test behavior.

The external_executor_id parameter is added but never used within EcsExecutorTask, suggesting it may be unnecessary or the implementation is incomplete.

The external_executor_id parameter is added to the EcsExecutorTask constructor and stored as an instance variable, but there's no code in the diff or visible in the class that actually uses this attribute. In the ECS executor context, the task_arn already serves as the unique identifier for ECS tasks, and external_executor_id is typically stored on TaskInstance objects, not on executor task objects. This parameter appears to be either unused or its purpose is unclear.

The queued state should be set before adding to active_workers to maintain consistent state ordering, as the task should be marked as queued before tracking it as an active worker.

Looking at how other executors (Kubernetes, Celery) handle this, they typically set the QUEUED state in the event_buffer before or as part of the execution flow. Currently, the task is added to active_workers first, then marked as queued. If an exception occurs between these two operations or if sync() runs between them, the task could be in active_workers without being properly marked as queued in the event buffer. Swapping the order would make the state transition more robust.

This should be `elif` instead of `if` to match the pattern used in `get_active_state` and other similar methods, since only one child state can exist at a time.

The conditional logic here is inconsistent with other similar methods in the codebase like `get_active_state` (line 547-553) and `_insert_tasks_into_state` (lines 575-601), which use `if/elif/elif` pattern. Since only one child state can be active at a time based on the `run_state`, line 539 should use `elif` instead of `if`. This bug could cause always_child_state to not be cleared when rescue_child_state exists but tasks_child_state doesn't.

Removing the NULL check introduces a potential segmentation fault. The original code `return cipher != NULL ? cipher->block_size : 0;` was defensive and prevented crashes when cipher is NULL. This change is unrelated to the CMAC mac-size fix and should be reverted.

This change removes critical NULL-safety from a public API function. While the fix in cmac_prov.c correctly addresses the segfault issue, this change introduces a new vulnerability in an unrelated function.

This test condition is confusing. Use `TEST_true(size_before_init != 0 || size_after_init != 0)` instead, which more clearly expresses that at least one size should be set.

The double negative `!TEST_false(...)` makes the test logic harder to understand. A positive assertion would be clearer and more maintainable.

With the NULL check removed from EVP_CIPHER_get_block_size(), this function will segfault if ctx->cipher is NULL. Add a NULL check: `if (ctx == NULL || ctx->cipher == NULL) return 0;`

The removal of the NULL check in EVP_CIPHER_get_block_size() creates a vulnerability here. This function should validate ctx->cipher before passing it to EVP_CIPHER_get_block_size().

Empty string should be included in validKVCacheTypes to avoid spurious warnings when using the default.

When OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE is not set, envconfig.KvCacheType() returns an empty string. This empty string is treated as valid (defaults to f16) in kvCacheBytesPerElement(), but SupportsKVCacheType() returns false for it. This causes a warning "kv cache type not supported by model" to be logged in server.go line 237 even when using the default, which is confusing for users. The valid types should include "" to match the behavior in kvCacheBytesPerElement().

When kvct is empty (default), the --kv-cache-type parameter should still be set to "f16" to be explicit.

Currently, when kvct is empty (the default case when OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE is not set), the --kv-cache-type parameter is not added to params at all. While llama.cpp defaults to f16, it's better to be explicit. After fixing SupportsKVCacheType to accept empty strings, this code should normalize empty string to "f16" before checking support, ensuring the parameter is always explicitly set when Flash Attention is enabled.

When an unsupported KV cache type is requested, a warning should be logged here like in server.go.

Currently, if flash attention is enabled but the requested KV cache type is not supported by the model, kvct silently remains empty and is passed to GraphSize(). While this works because empty defaults to f16, it's inconsistent with server.go which logs a warning in this case. Users should be informed when their requested cache type is being ignored.

If only X-Forwarded-Proto is set without X-Forwarded-Host, this will use req.Host (e.g., "gitea:3000"), exposing internal hostnames. Both headers should be required: change line 75-76 to `if reqScheme == "" || reqHost == "" {`.

The current logic checks if reqScheme is empty and returns setting.AppURL, but then allows reqHost to fall back to req.Host. This creates an edge case: if a misconfigured reverse proxy sets only X-Forwarded-Proto but not X-Forwarded-Host, the function will return something like "https://gitea:3000/sub/" using the internal container hostname. This is the exact problem from issue #31002 that this PR is trying to fix. The comment at line 68 states "The reverse proxy is configured correctly, it passes X-Forwarded-Proto/Host headers" (note the plural), suggesting both should be required together. The fix should require both headers to be present before using them.

Add a test case for when only X-Forwarded-Proto is set (without X-Forwarded-Host) to verify it returns cfg-host instead of exposing internal hostnames.

The tests cover the case where only X-Forwarded-Host is set (line 57-64), but there's no test for when only X-Forwarded-Proto is set without X-Forwarded-Host. This is an important edge case because a misconfigured reverse proxy might set only the protocol header. The test should verify that in this scenario, the function returns the configured AppURL rather than using req.Host (which could be an internal hostname like "gitea:3000"). This would help prevent regressions of issue #31002.

This assignment is incorrect. `espi_dev->data` is of type `struct espi_emul_data` (the controller), but `data_host` expects `struct espi_host_emul_data` (the emulator). This should be `data_host = emul_espi->target->data;`

The code incorrectly retrieves the controller's data instead of the emulator's data, causing a type mismatch that will lead to undefined behavior when accessing vw_state array.

The union pointers should be const-qualified since they point to statically allocated bus structures that shouldn't change after initialization (e.g., `const struct i2c_emul *i2c`).

The bus union members are initialized to point to static structures and shouldn't be modified at runtime, but the target field within those structures needs to be set during initialization, creating a const-correctness issue.

This assignment is redundant since `emul_init_for_bus()` in `subsys/emul/emul.c` already sets the target pointer (line 47). The init function should only initialize emulator-specific state.

The target pointer is set automatically by the framework in emul_init_for_bus before calling the emulator's init function, so this manual assignment is unnecessary code duplication.

Inconsistent spacing in the cast - there's a space before `shm` but no space in the same cast on line 222. Use consistent style throughout the file.

Code style should be consistent. The cast on line 222 is `(uintptr_t)data->shm_acpi_mmap` without a space, but this line has a space.

The parameter name was changed from `emul` to `target`, but the function is an init callback where the parameter represents the emulator being initialized. The name `emul` was more semantically accurate for this context than `target`.

While `target` makes sense for bus API callbacks (which receive the target device being emulated), init functions receive the emulator itself, so `emul` or `emulator` would be clearer parameter names.

The default case handles both invalid bus types and valid bus types with disabled configs, but the warning message only mentions disabled buses. Consider making the message more accurate.

The warning message should distinguish between an invalid bus_type value and a valid bus_type that lacks the corresponding CONFIG option.

Modifying data through a const pointer. The `emul` is `const struct emul *`, making this assignment to `emul->bus.i2c->target` potentially undefined behavior.

This writes to a structure member through a const-qualified pointer, which violates const correctness and may not compile or could cause undefined behavior.

Typo in array element: 'neight_int_ip' should be 'neigh_int_ip'.

The last element in the columns array contains a typo - 'neight_int_ip' should be 'neigh_int_ip' (neighbor interface IP).

The comment "this reads file in one go" is misleading since the loop actually reads the file multiple times (once per column).

The comment states the file is read "in one go", but the loop with the csvfile lookup actually reads the CSV file 7 times (once for each iteration). This is misleading to users. The comment should either be removed or clarified.

Workflow prompts should display the filename alongside the name (e.g., "Foo Flow (foo.yml)") as specified in the issue requirements, but only the workflow name is shown.

The custom instructions explicitly state that workflow prompts should show "Foo Flow (foo.yml)" format instead of just the name. The code at line 182 only appends `workflow.Name` instead of including the filename from the path.

The Use and Short fields incorrectly reference "run-id" and "workflow run" when this command views workflow definitions, not runs.

Lines 41-42 have misleading documentation. The command accepts a workflow ID/name and displays the workflow YAML file, not a workflow run. This should say something like "view [<workflow-id>]" and "View a workflow definition".

Missing newline after workflow ID line will cause the YAML content to appear on the same line.

Line 136 uses `fmt.Fprintf` without a trailing newline, so the subsequent markdown rendering will appear immediately after the ID on the same line. Should use `fmt.Fprintln` or add "\n" to the format string.

Setting err unconditionally when len(workflows) == 0 will overwrite any actual error from GetWorkflows.

Lines 164-165 check the length and set err without checking if err is already non-nil. If GetWorkflows returned an error AND zero workflows, the original error message would be replaced with "no workflows are enabled", losing valuable diagnostic information.

GitHub API responses often include whitespace in base64 content that should be stripped before decoding to prevent errors.

Line 69 should use `strings.TrimSpace(result.Content)` before base64 decoding, as the GitHub API commonly includes newlines or spaces in the content field which will cause base64 decoding to fail.

Disambiguation prompt should use the simpler "Foo Flow (foo.yml)" format as specified in the issue, not the verbose format with ID and full path.

The custom instructions indicate that prompts should consistently show "Foo Flow (foo.yml)" format instead of mixing formats. Line 238 uses a verbose format with ID and full path, which contradicts the desired simple filename-based format.

Infinite loop possible if API consistently returns empty pages after the first request.

The loop condition at line 40 only checks `len(workflows) < limit` but if the API returns 0 workflows on the first page, the loop breaks at line 58. However, if it returns some workflows on page 1 but 0 on subsequent pages due to an API bug, this could loop indefinitely since the break at line 58 only triggers when `len(result.Workflows) < perPage`, not when it's exactly 0.

The obj.get() call also needs **self.extra_args to handle encrypted objects with SSECustomerKey.

The read_key method has the same issue as get_key had - when reading encrypted S3 objects that require SSECustomerKey or SSECustomerAlgorithm parameters, the obj.get() call will fail with a 400 Bad Request error. According to boto3 documentation, S3.Object.get() accepts the same SSE parameters as head_object(). The line should be: return obj.get(**self.extra_args)['Body'].read().decode('utf-8')

Consider adding a test case to verify this works with SSECustomerKey and SSECustomerAlgorithm extra_args.

The fix addresses an important bug with encrypted S3 objects, but there's no test coverage to prevent regression. A test should verify that get_key() and download_file() work correctly when extra_args contains SSECustomerKey and SSECustomerAlgorithm parameters.

Add the `new` keyword when throwing this error for consistency with the rest of the codebase (52 of 55 error throws use `new`).

The codebase predominantly uses `new` when throwing FastifyError instances (52 out of 55 cases). In the same file, line 21 uses `throw new FST_ERR_LOG_INVALID_DESTINATION()`. This inconsistency should be fixed.

The error message should mention using `loggerInstance` to pass a logger instance, matching the pattern used in the documentation.

The documentation in Errors.md says "To pass an instance, use 'loggerInstance' instead." This helpful guidance should also be in the error message itself for better developer experience, similar to FST_ERR_LOG_INVALID_LOGGER_INSTANCE which mentions the correct usage.

Add the `new` keyword when throwing this error for consistency with the rest of the codebase (52 of 55 error throws use `new`).

The codebase predominantly uses `new` when throwing FastifyError instances (52 out of 55 cases). In the same file, line 21 uses `throw new FST_ERR_LOG_INVALID_DESTINATION()`. This inconsistency should be fixed.

Add a test case that uses both --no-annotate and --no-header flags together to verify they work correctly in combination.

The implementation adds two new independent flags (--no-annotate and --no-header), but there's no test verifying that both flags can be used together. This is an important use case since users may want to exclude both the header and annotations for minimal output.

The critical section is split when logging, which could allow ISR to set rx_buffer_full_flg=true between the exit and re-enter, then this code immediately clears it.

When rx_buffered_len != 0, the critical section exits to log, then re-enters. During this gap, the ISR could validly set rx_buffer_full_flg=true, which would then be incorrectly cleared. Consider capturing the error state before exiting the critical section and logging after the final exit to maintain atomicity.

The comment on line 898 is incomplete and appears to be cut off mid-sentence.

This incomplete comment should either be removed or completed. It looks like it was intended to explain an alternative consideration but was left unfinished.

The function incorrectly matches lines starting with ':' but missing the closing colon, like `:name` instead of `:name: value`.

Since the first character is already checked to be ':', the `any` will always return true for the first character. To properly detect RST field list options (`:name: value`), the check should verify there's a second ':' after the first one. Consider using `line.chars().skip(1).take_while(|ch| !ch.is_whitespace()).any(|ch| ch == ':')`.

Tab trimming may remove more indentation than intended when `seen_indent_len + tab_expansion > indent_len`.

When a tab is encountered and expanding it would exceed `indent_len`, the entire tab is removed. This could strip more indentation than `indent_len` specifies. For example, if `indent_len=5` and `seen_indent_len=0`, a tab would add 8 to reach the next tab stop, but the entire tab gets removed even though we only wanted to trim 5 characters worth of indentation. The comment on line 543-545 in the test file acknowledges this behavior, but it may lead to unexpected results.

The regex `::(?s:.*)` is overly aggressive and may strip legitimate content containing `::` that isn't a reStructuredText block.

This regex will match and remove everything after the first occurrence of `::` in a docstring, even if it's not actually a reStructuredText literal block. For example, a docstring containing "dict :: str -> int" or URL "https://example.com" would have content incorrectly stripped. While the comment acknowledges this is a "hammer" approach, it could lead to false positives in test normalization where valid non-RST content is removed.

Remove commented-out debug code before merging.

The commented-out debug code with DebugBreak() should be removed as it serves no purpose in the final version and clutters the test code.

Use `bufferSize.EndExclusive()` instead of `GetSize().EndExclusive()` since bufferSize is already defined.

The bufferSize variable is already initialized on line 1362, so this check should use bufferSize.EndExclusive() for consistency and to avoid redundant function calls.

This assertion on lines 1055-1058 is a duplicate of lines 1051-1054.

The test contains duplicate assertions testing the exact same condition twice in a row, which provides no additional value and should be removed.

This assertion on lines 473-476 is a duplicate of lines 469-472.

The test contains duplicate assertions testing the exact same condition twice in a row, which provides no additional value and should be removed.

This assertion on lines 398-401 is a duplicate of lines 394-397.

The test contains duplicate assertions testing the exact same condition twice in a row, which provides no additional value and should be removed.

Deadlock: disk_access_get_di() acquires the spinlock, and then line 184 tries to acquire it again, causing a deadlock since spinlocks are not recursive.

This is a critical bug. The function disk_access_get_di() already acquires the spinlock on line 31, and attempting to lock it again on line 184 will cause a deadlock. The validation check needs to be done without calling disk_access_get_di(), or the code needs to be restructured to avoid double-locking.

Deadlock: disk_access_get_di() acquires the spinlock, and then line 206 tries to acquire it again, causing a deadlock since spinlocks are not recursive.

This is a critical bug. The function disk_access_get_di() already acquires the spinlock on line 31, and attempting to lock it again on line 206 will cause a deadlock. The validation check needs to be done without calling disk_access_get_di(), or the code needs to be restructured to avoid double-locking.

Race condition: refcnt is accessed and modified without holding the spinlock, allowing concurrent modifications.

After getting the disk info pointer, the spinlock is released by disk_access_get_di(). The subsequent reads and writes to disk->refcnt (lines 62, 68, 71, 73) are not protected, creating a race condition where multiple threads could simultaneously modify refcnt in ISR context.

Race condition: refcnt is accessed and modified without holding the spinlock, allowing concurrent modifications.

After getting the disk info pointer, the spinlock is released by disk_access_get_di(). The subsequent reads and writes to disk->refcnt throughout the ioctl function are not protected, creating a race condition where multiple threads could simultaneously modify refcnt in ISR context.

Logging should not be done while holding a spinlock as it can be expensive and may not be safe in interrupt context.

The LOG_DBG call on line 187 is executed while holding the spinlock. Logging operations can be expensive and might involve operations incompatible with spinlock constraints. Move the logging statement after k_spin_unlock().

The spinlock must be initialized to zero; consider using = {0} for explicit initialization.

While static variables are zero-initialized by default in C, spinlocks should be explicitly initialized to ensure proper state. Based on the spinlock.h header, the struct should be zero-initialized, but it's better to be explicit with = {0} or verify this matches the expected initialization pattern for spinlocks in Zephyr.

Creating a new bitmap and brush on every call is inefficient. Consider caching these brushes as static members or in a map keyed by shape to avoid recreating D2D resources repeatedly.

The createShadedBitmapBrush function creates new D2D1Bitmap and D2D1BitmapBrush objects on every invocation. These are expensive GPU resources that should ideally be cached and reused. While the performance impact might be acceptable for infrequent glyph rendering, this pattern could cause performance degradation when rendering many shaded glyphs.

The position calculations now use relative coordinates before adding rectX/rectY, but ellipse shapes still use endX/endY as radii rather than coordinates, which could cause incorrect rendering.

In the FilledEllipsis and EmptyEllipsis cases, endX and endY are used directly as the radii for the ellipse. However, with the refactoring that moved position calculation to relative coordinates, these values are now relative to the cell origin (0-1 range scaled by cell dimensions) before being converted to absolute. The ellipse center uses begXabs/begYabs correctly, but the radii are still using the transformed endX/endY values which are no longer in rectX coordinate space. This could lead to incorrect ellipse sizing.

The comment states glyphIndices stores UTF16 when fontFace is nullptr, but this is misleading. It actually stores UTF-32 codepoints split across surrogate pairs in the u16 array.

The comment says "this stores UTF16 because we're dealing with a custom glyph" but this is imprecise. Looking at the code in _flushBufferLine and BackendD3D.cpp, when dealing with custom glyphs (soft fonts), the glyphIndices array can store surrogate pairs that need to be combined to form UTF-32 codepoints. The comment should clarify that it stores UTF-16 encoded codepoints (potentially as surrogate pairs) rather than just "UTF16".

This field should be `AtomicBool` instead of `bool` for consistency with other dynamically changeable instance variables like `TiDBEnableDDL` and `CheckMb4ValueInUTF8`.

Looking at other instance-scoped variables that can be modified at runtime (TiDBEnableDDL, CheckMb4ValueInUTF8), they use AtomicBool type in the config struct. This allows thread-safe access and consistent updating between the config struct and the runtime atomic variable. Since TiDBRCReadCheckTS can be changed via SET GLOBAL at runtime, it should follow the same pattern.

The SetGlobal function should also update `config.GetGlobalConfig().Instance.TiDBRCReadCheckTS` to maintain consistency between the config struct and the atomic variable, following the pattern of `TiDBEnableDDL` and `TiDBCheckMb4ValueInUTF8`.

Other instance-scoped variables like TiDBEnableDDL (line 449) and TiDBCheckMb4ValueInUTF8 (line 381) update both the atomic variable and the config struct value in their SetGlobal functions. This ensures consistency between the config and runtime state. The TiDBRCReadCheckTS should follow the same pattern.

The `Value` field should read from `config.GetGlobalConfig().Instance.TiDBRCReadCheckTS` instead of `DefRCReadCheckTS` to reflect the actual configured value from the config file, consistent with `TiDBEnableDDL`.

The TiDBEnableDDL variable uses `Value: BoolToOnOff(config.GetGlobalConfig().Instance.TiDBEnableDDL.Load())` (line 444) to initialize from the config file value. Since TiDBRCReadCheckTS is also an instance-level config that can be set in the config file, it should follow the same pattern to respect the configured value on startup.

The `world_size > 1` check is redundant since `reduce_from_tensor_model_parallel_region` already checks this internally (see `_reduce` in mappings.py line 18).

This is an observation about redundant checking. While the added check provides a minor optimization by avoiding a function call, the `_reduce` function in mappings.py already has the same check at line 18: `if get_tensor_model_parallel_world_size()==1: return input_`. The optimization is valid but creates duplicate logic.

After waiting for overlapped IO to complete, verify the write succeeded using GetOverlappedResult to check for errors and get bytes written.

The code waits for the overlapped IO event but doesn't verify the operation succeeded. GetOverlappedResult should be called to check for errors and potentially retrieve the number of bytes written. Without this, silent write failures could occur.

This const_cast violates const-correctness and could cause undefined behavior if the source buffer is actually const.

Modifying data through a const_cast is undefined behavior unless the original object is non-const. While the comment claims the backing memory comes from _inputBuffer, this assumption is fragile and could break if the call chain changes. Consider copying the data when control characters need replacement, or use a different approach that doesn't mutate const data.

Good fix - the original comparison was inverted (< instead of >).

This corrects a logic error where the validation was checking if targetArea is less than the buffer size, when it should be checking if it's greater. This would have allowed buffer overruns in the original code.

The variable name should be `dwNewMode` instead of `mode` for consistency with the ENABLE_WRAP_AT_EOL_OUTPUT check above.

The code uses `mode` here but `dwNewMode` in the ENABLE_WRAP_AT_EOL_OUTPUT block above. This inconsistency could indicate a bug or at minimum makes the code harder to understand. Both should use the same variable for the new mode value.

Emitting " \r" will overwrite the first column of the next row, corrupting output as mentioned in the PR description unresolved quirks.

The PR description states this breaks text reflow on window resize and notes "We cannot emit ` \r` the way readline does it, because this would overwrite the first column in the next row with a whitespace." Yet this code does exactly that, which will corrupt the display.

Trailing byte should be marked with COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE, not COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE.

The trailing half of a wide glyph is incorrectly marked with COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE when it should be COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE. This will cause incorrect rendering and processing of wide characters.

Trailing half should set COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE, not COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE.

The trailing half of a wide character is incorrectly marked with COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE. This will break wide character rendering and cause text corruption. The line should OR with COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE instead.

Calling Min() and Max() will throw InvalidOperationException if indices is empty (when TilesCount is 0), causing a crash instead of a clear error message.

The LINQ Min() and Max() methods throw InvalidOperationException when called on an empty sequence. If a template has TilesCount == 0 (e.g., Size: 0,0 or an empty PickAny template), this will crash with an unhelpful exception instead of the intended validation error message.

This changes the default indices from frameCount to TilesCount, which may cause incorrect behavior when the template size doesn't match the sprite dimensions.

The original code used Enumerable.Range(0, frameCount) to generate indices based on the actual sprite's frame count. The new code uses TilesCount (template size), which is a different value. This means if TilesCount > frameCount, the validation will correctly catch it, but if TilesCount < frameCount, some sprite frames may be silently ignored when they should be used.

Missing copyright header. All Java source files in this codebase should start with the standard Apache 2.0 license header.

This is a new file that's missing the copyright header that's present in all other Java files in the codebase. This is a code quality and legal compliance issue.

Fields should be `private final` for proper encapsulation and immutability guarantees.

These AtomicInteger fields are never reassigned and should be private final to follow Java best practices. The ThreadSafe annotation implies these fields should be properly encapsulated.

Method name should be `summary()` following Java naming conventions (methods start with lowercase).

Java method naming conventions require methods to start with lowercase letters. This is used in RemoteModule.java as `stats.Summary()` which should be `stats.summary()`.

Missing space after `if` - should be `if (fallback > 0)`.

This violates the codebase's consistent spacing convention seen throughout other files.

Unused import - `Joiner` is never used in this file.

This import was added but is not used anywhere in the code.

Missing space after `if` - should be `if (stats != null)`.

This violates the codebase's consistent spacing convention, as seen in other null checks in this same file (lines 117, 121, 167).

Stats are output even when remote execution is not enabled. Add a null check or condition to only output stats when `stats` is not null and remote is actually enabled.

The buildComplete method will always be called, but stats might be null if remote execution is not configured. This could cause a NullPointerException.

Duplicate function name - two functions named `test_remote_stats` will cause the second to override the first.

There are two test functions with the same name at lines 513 and 571. The second one should have a different name like `test_remote_stats_fallback`.

Remove the empty line added at the start of the file.

An empty line was added at line 1 before the shebang, which is incorrect and could cause the script to fail.

Missing stats tracking - when remote executor is null, execution goes local but no stat is incremented, causing this to be incorrectly counted as fallback.

When remoteExecutor is null but remoteCache exists, the spawn executes locally but stats are not updated. This case will be incorrectly classified as "fallback" in the Summary calculation, when it's actually intentional local execution due to cache-only mode.

The upper bound should be 999.0 since lrint() rounds and could produce 1001 for values near 1000.5.

Using lrint() for rounding means that values >= 1000.0 could round to 1001, which exceeds the valid uint16_t range after gsl::narrow_cast. The condition should be `value >= 1.0 && value < 1000.0` or the upper bound should be adjusted to account for rounding behavior.

The temporary shared_ptr from lock() is destroyed immediately, making mostRecent a dangling pointer; assign to a shared_ptr first.

Calling `.lock().get()` creates a temporary `shared_ptr` that is destroyed at the end of the statement, leaving `mostRecent` as a raw pointer to potentially freed memory. This should be: `auto mostRecentShared = mostRecentWeak.lock(); auto mostRecent = mostRecentShared.get();` or simply work with the shared_ptr directly.

Replace `.ToDictionary(player => player.Name).Values` with `.ToList()` to avoid creating an unnecessary intermediate dictionary.

The current code creates a dictionary just to extract its values, which is inefficient. Since the result is stored in a ValueCollection variable, it would be better to use ToList() or ToArray() instead. This eliminates the overhead of dictionary creation and provides the same IEnumerable interface needed by ShowDropDown.

The dropdown should also be disabled when owners.Count == 0 to prevent showing an empty dropdown.

The code only disables the dropdown when there's exactly one owner, but doesn't handle the case where there are zero valid owners. If owners.Count == 0, the dropdown remains enabled but clicking it would show an empty list, which is poor UX.

Use `IEnumerable<PlayerReference>` instead of the concrete `Dictionary<string, PlayerReference>.ValueCollection` type for better flexibility.

The variable type should be the interface IEnumerable<PlayerReference> rather than the specific ValueCollection type, which provides better abstraction and works with any collection type including List, Array, or ValueCollection.

If the actor's current owner is not in the filtered owners list, it should be updated to a valid owner to prevent invalid state.

When RequiresSpecificOwners filters the owner list, the actor's current owner might not be in the valid owners list (e.g., if the map was manually edited). The code should validate and update selectedOwner after filtering to ensure it's always a valid choice from the filtered list.

The lambda expression formatting is inconsistent and less readable. Keep the lambda on the same line: `(InferredJsonClass fromBody) => { })`.

The formatting change splits the lambda expression awkwardly across lines, making it less readable compared to the original single-line format. This is an unnecessary stylistic change that reduces code quality.

The lambda expression formatting is inconsistent and less readable. Keep the lambda on the same line as the attribute: `() => { })`.

The formatting change splits the lambda expression awkwardly across lines, making it less readable compared to the original single-line format. This is an unnecessary stylistic change that reduces code quality.

The calculation of `size_to_copy` is incorrect. It should be `net_buf_max_len(ext_scan_buf) - ext_scan_buf->len` to calculate the remaining space in the buffer, not `net_buf_max_len(ext_scan_buf) - evt->length`.

When truncating data because the buffer is full, the code needs to calculate how much space remains in the buffer. The remaining space is the maximum buffer size minus the current length of data already in the buffer (ext_scan_buf->len). The current code incorrectly subtracts evt->length instead of ext_scan_buf->len, which would result in copying the wrong amount of data or potentially causing buffer overflows/underflows.

The condition is inverted. According to line 565, BIT(5) set means "more data to come". So this should be `if (truncate && (evt->evt_type & BIT(5)))` to discard remaining fragments when we've truncated data AND the controller will send more.

The code should enter "discard mode" (set ext_scan_buf to NULL) when the host has truncated the data AND the controller indicates more data is coming (BIT(5) set). The current condition `!(evt->evt_type & BIT(5))` checks for the opposite case where NO more data is coming, which means we would never discard the remaining fragments when needed. The comment at line 565 confirms that BIT(5) set means "More data to come", so the condition at line 589 is backwards.

This should pass `null` instead of `new ApplicationException()` to match the fix applied at line 170.

The diff shows that line 170 was changed from `new ApplicationException()` to `null`, but line 83 (in the OnChange callback) still creates a new ApplicationException. Both calls should be consistent and pass null since no actual exception occurred - this is just a warning log about reaching the file limit.

String interpolation defeats the performance benefits of LoggerMessage.Define, which requires a compile-time constant format string.

LoggerMessage.Define is designed for high-performance logging by pre-compiling the message template. Using string interpolation here ($"...{variable}...") evaluates at runtime and creates a different string each time, negating the performance optimization. The message should either use a hardcoded string or use LoggerMessage.Define<T> with a placeholder like "Limit of {MaxFileCount} files per day has been reached" and pass MaxFileCount as a parameter.

This will throw NullReferenceException if OnChange was never called before InvokeChanged.

The _listener field is initialized to null and only set when OnChange is called. If InvokeChanged is called before OnChange, or if OnChange is never called, _listener will be null and invoking it will throw a NullReferenceException. Add a null check: `_listener?.Invoke(CurrentValue, null);`

The unique_id must be unique per entity. The thermostat entity already uses `self.thermostat["identifier"]`, so this creates a duplicate unique_id conflict. Add a suffix like `-humidifier` to make it unique.

This is a critical bug. The unique_id for the humidifier is now the same as the unique_id for the thermostat (see climate.py line 352), which will cause entity conflicts in Home Assistant. The previous implementation had `-humidifier` suffix which was correct.

The mock thermostat data is missing the "identifier" and "modelNumber" fields which are required by the humidifier's unique_id and device_info properties.

The test fixture should include all required fields. The humidifier accesses self.thermostat['identifier'] and self.thermostat['modelNumber'], but these fields are missing from the mock data, which means tests won't properly validate the actual implementation.

Pass an integer instead of a string to set_humidity to match the expected parameter type.

The set_humidity method should receive an integer value for humidity. While the current implementation passes it through without validation, this test should use the correct type (40 instead of "40") to properly test the expected behavior.

Add input validation to ensure humidity is an integer in the range 0-100, similar to the climate entity's set_humidity method.

The climate.py implementation validates humidity input (lines 697-701). The humidifier should have the same validation to prevent invalid values from being passed to the API.

Restore the error logging for unrecognized model numbers to maintain consistency with climate.py and sensor.py, which both log this issue for troubleshooting.

The removed error logging (lines 63-70 in the diff) is useful for users and developers to identify and report unrecognized thermostat models. This is logged in both climate.py (lines 360-367) and sensor.py (lines 81-88). Removing it here creates an inconsistency and makes debugging harder.

Add assertions for unique_id and device_info properties since those are the main additions in this PR.

The PR adds unique_id and device_info properties to the humidifier entity, but there are no tests validating these new properties work correctly. Tests should verify both properties return expected values.

Use `MarkerTree::from_str(s)` instead of `MarkerTree::parse_str(s)` to avoid type parameter ambiguity and match the `FromStr` trait's error type.

The method `parse_str` is generic with signature `pub fn parse_str<T: Pep508Url>(markers: &str) -> Result<Self, Pep508Error<T>>`, which requires a type parameter and returns a generic error type. However, the `FromStr` trait expects `Result<Self, Pep508Error>` (non-generic). Using `MarkerTree::from_str(s)` is the idiomatic approach for `FromStr` implementations and avoids potential type inference issues.

When `resolutions` has exactly one element and it's a Fork variant, directly return `vec![resolution.markers.fork_markers().cloned()]` instead of iterating over all resolutions, since there's only one.

The code pattern `if let [resolution] = resolutions` ensures there's exactly one resolution, but the implementation then iterates over `resolutions.iter()` to extract fork markers. This is redundant - we can directly extract the fork marker from the single resolution. The current code works but is unnecessarily complex for the single-element case.

Use `environments.map(|e| e.as_markers().to_vec()).unwrap_or_default()` to avoid cloning the entire struct.

Same issue as above - cloning the entire `SupportedEnvironments` struct is unnecessary when we only need to clone the inner vector. Using `as_markers().to_vec()` is more efficient.

Use `environments.map(|e| e.as_markers().to_vec()).unwrap_or_default()` to avoid cloning the entire struct.

Currently, the code clones the entire `SupportedEnvironments` struct and then calls `into_markers()` to extract the inner `Vec<MarkerTree>`. Since `environments` is `Option<&SupportedEnvironments>`, we can instead use `as_markers()` which returns `&[MarkerTree]`, then call `to_vec()` to clone only the vector contents. This is more efficient as it avoids cloning the wrapper struct.

Critical memory bug: srcBitmap is recycled here, but it's still referenced in the supplier within seekbarPreviewData and will be called again on subsequent seekbar changes, causing crashes.

The bitmap returned from `getBitmapAt()` is generated by a Supplier that creates it from a cached source bitmap. Recycling it here means the source bitmap in `SeekbarPreviewThumbnailHolder.generateDataFrom()` (line 129: `final Bitmap srcBitMap = getBitMapFrom(url)`) is destroyed while still being referenced by the lambda at line 153-155. This will cause crashes when the seekbar is moved again.

Thread-safety issue: currentUpdateRequestIdentifier is accessed from multiple threads but not declared volatile, which can cause visibility problems.

This field is written in `resetFrom()` (main thread) and read in `resetFromAsync()` (background thread). Without volatile, the background thread may not see the updated value, defeating the cancellation mechanism.

Memory leak: Bitmaps stored in suppliers are never recycled before clearing the map, causing memory to accumulate.

The seekbarPreviewData map contains suppliers that hold references to downloaded bitmaps (srcBitMap from line 129). These bitmaps should be recycled before clearing the map to prevent memory leaks, especially when switching between videos.

Memory leak: seekbarPreviewThumbnailHolder is never cleaned up, leaving bitmaps and background threads potentially running.

The seekbarPreviewThumbnailHolder contains bitmaps and may have background executors running. A cleanup method should be called here to recycle bitmaps and ensure executor services are shut down properly.

Executor services are not tracked, making it impossible to ensure all are shut down when the player is destroyed or when rapid video changes occur.

Each call to resetFrom() creates a new ExecutorService, but there's no mechanism to track and properly shut down all created executors. This could lead to thread leaks, especially when quickly switching between videos.

Potential NullPointerException if getThumb() returns null, which can happen in some Android versions or custom themes.

While there's a try-catch around this code, it would be safer to explicitly check for null before calling getBounds() to avoid unnecessary exception handling in the normal flow.

Redundant null check: srcBitmap is already verified by checking optPreviewThumbnail.isPresent() above, making requireNonNull unnecessary.

The Optional.isPresent() check guarantees that get() will not return null, so the Objects.requireNonNull() is redundant and adds no value.

Typo in log message: "heigh" should be "height".

Minor typo that should be corrected for consistency and clarity in debug logs.

This breaks the original behavior where currentDisplaySeek was updated regardless of fromUser, which may cause UI inconsistencies.

The original code only logged when fromUser was true but always updated currentDisplaySeek. This change means programmatic seekbar updates won't update the display text, which could be intentional but should be verified as it changes existing behavior.

Each call to the supplier creates a new bitmap but these new bitmaps are never recycled, causing memory leaks on every seekbar change.

Every time the user moves the seekbar, getBitmapAt() executes this supplier which creates a new bitmap via Bitmap.createBitmap(). These new bitmaps are passed to tryResizeAndSetSeekbarPreviewThumbnail where they're recycled, but this recycles the wrong bitmap (see other comment). The system needs to track and properly recycle both the source bitmaps and the derived bitmaps.

This should be `new_path = arg[0]` not `arg[1]`. The tuple format is (new_path, old_path), so arg[0] is the new path and arg[1] is the old path. This bug causes incorrect suggestions for renames like FSHook, FileSensor, and other contrib classes moving to core airflow.operators/sensors/hooks paths.

The variable is named `new_path` but is assigned `arg[1]` which is actually the OLD path from the tuple. The tuples in the ALL list are structured as `(new_path, old_path)`, so to check the NEW path (which doesn't exist in 1.10.x), we need to check `arg[0]`. This bug causes 5 cases where the filter incorrectly includes renames that would break DAGs on 1.10.x, such as suggesting airflow.sensors.filesystem.FileSensor which doesn't exist in 1.10.x.

The SEEN_DIP and WARN_DIP sets will grow unbounded without cleanup, potentially causing memory issues in long-running instances with many sensors.

The implementation creates persistent sets (SEEN_DIP and WARN_DIP) in hass.data that accumulate entity IDs but are never cleared. While this follows the existing pattern for WARN_UNSUPPORTED_UNIT and WARN_UNSTABLE_UNIT, it means that for every sensor that experiences a dip, its entity_id remains in memory permanently. In long-running Home Assistant instances with many sensors, this could accumulate thousands of entity IDs over time, leading to a gradual memory leak.

Consider using `if (module.used !== true)` instead of `if (!module.used)` for clarity, as the current condition is also true when `module.used === false`.

The condition `!module.used` is truthy when `module.used` is `false`, `undefined`, `null`, `0`, or `""`. While functionally correct, using `module.used !== true` would more clearly express the intent to only set `false` when not explicitly marked as `true`, avoiding the redundant assignment when already `false`.

A `ConsoleLogDestination` class already exists in `Duplicati/Agent/ConsoleLogDestination.cs`. Consider reusing that class or consolidating the implementations to avoid code duplication.

There's code duplication - the same class exists in two locations with slightly different implementations. The Agent version doesn't filter by level, while this Server version does. This violates DRY principles and could lead to maintenance issues.

Remove the extra blank line after the constant declarations for consistency with the surrounding code style.

There's an unnecessary blank line after the LOG_CONSOLE_OPTION constant that creates inconsistent spacing in the constants section.

This logic will produce false positives by flagging any call to `match?` or `include?` on any method chain, not just exception messages. It should verify the receiver is calling `.message` (e.g., `condition_node.condition.receiver.method_name == :message`).

The current implementation only checks if the method is `match?` or `include?` and if it's called on a send node, but doesn't verify that the receiver is actually calling `.message`.
